coredata.dc:title: Effect of aggregation configuration of molecular fluorophore CZA on photoluminescence properties of carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: The effect of aggregation configuration of molecular fluorophore citrazinic acid (CZA) on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of carbon dots (CDs) has been investigated using first-principles method. The structural stability of all aggregates has been analyzed, and the results show that the most stable structures are J-type CZA aggregates with head-to-tail configurations and the CZA/CD aggregates are bonded by replacing H atoms on the CD edges with de-OH from the pyridine ring of CZA. The luminescent properties of CZA/CD aggregates are mainly affected by the binding modes and binding sites. When the sites belong to electron-donating groups, electron-withdrawing groups or sp2 domain, the PL spectra of CDs are shifted and the luminescent intensities are significantly enhanced. The results suggest that covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates are responsible for the high fluorescence quantum yield of CD. Moreover, the distance between the centers of the two pyridine rings in H-type CZA dimers less than 3.5 Å is prone to π-π stacking, leading to fluorescence quenching of aggregates. The present work is helpful in understanding the effect of molecular fluorophores on the PL properties of CDs and provides theoretical guidance for the controllable synthesis of CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized dimers and trimers of CZA. The distances between CZA molecules are indicated (unit: Å), where red dashed lines represent the formed hydrogen bonds. Here, white sphere represents H atom, gray sphere represents C atom, red sphere represents O atom, and blue sphere represents N atom.

Different aggregation systems of CZA/CD: (a) covalently bonded and (b) noncovalently bonded systems. The distance between CZA and CD is indicated (unit: Å), where the red dashed line indicates hydrogen bonding (white sphere = H atom, gray sphere = C atom, red sphere = O atom, and blue sphere = N atom).

Calculated PL spectra of (a) CZA aggregates, (b) noncovalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates, (c) covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates at binding sites “1″, ”4″ and “9″ and (d) covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates at binding sites ”8″ and “10″. The insets show a magnified view of selected regions of PL spectra. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission bands is assumed to be 0.5 eV.
Frontier molecular orbital energy levels of CZA aggregation structures (isovalue = 0.03). Numbers within the arrows are the energy gaps (E g, in eV) between HOMO and LUMO.
Frontier molecular orbital energy levels of noncovalently bonded CZA/CD aggregation structures (isovalue = 0.03). Numbers within the arrows are the energy gaps (E g, in eV) between HOMO and LUMO.

Frontier molecular orbital energy levels of (a) covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregation structures with main binding sites “1″, ”4″, “9″ and (d) covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregation structures with binding sites ”8″, “10″. Numbers within the arrows are the energy gaps (E g, in eV) between HOMO and LUMO.

Total transition dipole moments of CZA/CD aggregation structures. Green arrows indicate total transition dipole moments, red arrows indicate fragment transition dipole moment, and fragment division is indicated by a dashed line.

IGMH analysis for dimers with different levels of stacking. The coloring method of sign(λ2)ρ in different value ranges is given in Fig. S4. The non-bonded distances between CZA molecules are indicated and the unit is Å.
Calculated PL spectra and frontier molecular orbital energy levels of the dimers with different levels of stacking.
Transition dipole moment of four CZA dimers. The red arrow indicates the transition dipole moment of the individual CZA, and the green arrows describe the total transition dipole moment of the dimer.
Cohesion energies of different CZA and CZA/CD aggregation systems.

head.ce:title: Effect of aggregation configuration of molecular fluorophore CZA on photoluminescence properties of carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: First-principles calculations reveal that CZA aggregates and CZA/CD aggregates are responsible for blue emission and green emission, respectively. The J-type CZA aggregates in head-to-tail configuration are the most stable, while the H-type aggregates are prone to π-π stacking, resulting in fluorescence quenching. For CZA/CD aggregates, the bonding modes and sites have a significant impact on the photoluminescence properties of CDs. When the bonding sites belong to electron-donating groups, electron-withdrawing groups or sp2 domain, the luminescent intensities of CDs are significantly enhanced.

The effect of aggregation configuration of molecular fluorophore citrazinic acid (CZA) on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of carbon dots (CDs) has been investigated using first-principles method. The structural stability of all aggregates has been analyzed, and the results show that the most stable structures are J-type CZA aggregates with head-to-tail configurations and the CZA/CD aggregates are bonded by replacing H atoms on the CD edges with de-OH from the pyridine ring of CZA. The luminescent properties of CZA/CD aggregates are mainly affected by the binding modes and binding sites. When the sites belong to electron-donating groups, electron-withdrawing groups or sp2 domain, the PL spectra of CDs are shifted and the luminescent intensities are significantly enhanced. The results suggest that covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates are responsible for the high fluorescence quantum yield of CD. Moreover, the distance between the centers of the two pyridine rings in H-type CZA dimers less than 3.5 Å is prone to π-π stacking, leading to fluorescence quenching of aggregates. The present work is helpful in understanding the effect of molecular fluorophores on the PL properties of CDs and provides theoretical guidance for the controllable synthesis of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) [1,2] nanoparticles have excellent optical and electronic properties, and have attracted considerable research interest in recent years. Compared to other zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs possess numerous advantages. These include high fluorescent quantum yields (QYs), simple preparation, high stability and better biocompatibility. They can be applied in multiple potential applications such as in bioimaging, LED lighting, energy conversion, and photocatalysis [3–9]. Synthesis methods for CDs are usually categorized into top-down and bottom-up methods [10]. The luminescence of CDs can be attributed to three sources: core states, surface states, and molecular states. Core states mean the sp2 hybridization domains within the CDs; surface states refer to surface functional groups containing N or O; molecular states refer to molecular fluorophores (MFs) generated during the synthesis of the CDs [11–14]. In general, the core states derived from the π-π* transition in CDs produce blue emission, and its luminescence properties are affected by the quantum size effect; the surface states from the n-π* transition produce green emission [15,16]. Recently, by using bottom-up thermal decomposition method, CDs synthesized from citric acid (CA) and nitrogen sources exhibit ultra-high fluorescence QYs, which originates from the generation of extra fluorescence emitting centers, namely MFs, during the synthesis process [17–20]. At present, many MFs have been investigated, including citrazinic acid (CZA) [21], 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c] pyridine-1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione (HPPT) [22], 1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo[1,2-α] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) [23], 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) [24], and more. The earliest synthesis of CZA can be traced back to Bellman and Hoffman's study of the reaction between CA and ammonia in 1884 [25]. In 1893, CZA was successfully prepared using CA and urea at 130 °C [26]. In recent years, cheap and easily-processed CA and urea are often used as precursors to synthesize CDs, and thus CZA molecules are inevitably generated in this process. Many experiments have demonstrated that the molecular fluorophore CZA can make a significant contribution to the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs [21,27]. In order to better design and synthesize high-performance CDs, increasing attention has been paid to the luminescent effects of MFs on CDs [28,29]. Schneider et al. [30] found that amorphous CDs prepared from CA and ammonia displayed blue-green dual emission due to the aggregation of CZA, but at the same time, they also found that CDs were susceptible to fluorescence quenching due to more aggregation of CZA. Rahul et al. [31,32] found that the CDs synthesized from CA and urea yielded dual emission centers of CZA (blue luminescence domains) and HPPT (green luminescence domains) and also there existed an interaction between the two emission domains. Otten et al. [33] synthesized four types of CDs containing CZA molecules by four different routes using CA and urea as precursors, and suggested that the strong interaction between CZA and CDs was responsible for the generation of abnormally high QYs in CDs.

In order to reveal the relationship between molecular fluorophores CZA and the PL properties of CDs, some theoretical works have been carried out. Cappai et al. [34] performed density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate CZA dimers and multimers, and found that the formation of aggregates was one of the main factors affecting the absorption and emission properties of CDs. Kudelev et al. [35] found molecular-like centers existed in blue-emitting CDs via DFT calculations, and the covalent and non-covalent couplings between these fluorescent centers resulted in the red-shifted fluorescence spectra and reduced the luminescence intensity. Langer et al. [36] carried out DFT calculations on the charge and energy transfer between carbon cores and individual MFs, and supported the viewpoint that an interaction existed between carbon cores, surfaces, and MFs.

Based on the experimental and theoretical works mentioned above, it is challenging to explain the mechanisms behind the enhancement of the PL intensity and induction of dual emission in CDs by MFs such as CZA. Also, there is still a debate regarding the existence state of CZA in CD solutions. Some experiments found that CZA molecules existed freely in solutions [31,37], while others indicate that CZA molecules were bound to CDs [32,38]. When CZA molecules were in free state, they could aggregate into dimers or multimers with different configurations [34,39,40]; when CZA molecules were bound to CDs, the two structures could be covalently or noncovalently bonded [33,38]. Since the optical properties of CDs are noticeably affected by CZA [33,41], it is crucial to investigate the correlation between the PL properties of CDs and the diverse aggregation configurations of CZA.

In this work, a series of CZA dimers and trimers, along with covalent and noncovalent aggregation structures of CZA and CDs have been constructed. The PL spectra and electronic properties of all aggregates are calculated using DFT and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) [42]. This work emphasizes the correlation between the configurations of aggregates and the PL properties of CDs in order to reveal the underlying mechanism of the enhanced PL intensity, the dual emission, as well as the fluorescence quenching.

All calculations of structures, electronic properties and PL spectra were performed using Gaussian program package [43]. All calculations were performed using the range-separated hybrid functions ωB97X-D with dispersion correction [44–46] and 6-311G(d) basis sets [47], under the aqueous solution based on polarizable continuum model (PCM) [48]. The stacked H-type structures and linear J-type structures have been experimentally confirmed to be the major configurations of CZA in solutions [34]. Therefore, a series of H-type and J-type CZA dimers, trimers, and CZA/CD aggregates were constructed to study the effects of aggregation configurations on the PL properties, and all initial structures are shown in Fig. S1. Firstly, all the aggregates were optimized using DFT method to obtain ground-state structures without imaginary frequencies. Then, the excited-state structures were optimized based on the ground-state structures, and linear response radiative transitions were performed using TD-DFT calculations to obtain the emission energies and oscillator strengths for describing PL spectra. The excited state properties of all structures were analyzed using the Multiwfn package [49], and all isosurface plots were drawn using the VMD visualization program [50].

There are eight aggregation configurations for CZA, including H-type dimer 1, dimer 2 and trimer 1, and J-type dimer 3, dimer 4, dimer 5, dimer 6 and trimer 2. The optimized H-type and J-type structures are shown in Fig. 1 . The initial H-type structure comprises CZA molecules stacked in parallel planes with an interplanar distance of 3 to 4 Å, as indicated in Fig. S1. Dimer 1 exhibits a typical H-type anti-mirror stack structure. It is initially constructed by stacking two CZA molecules, one of which is rotated by 180° around the y-axis relative to the other. After structural optimization, as depicted in Fig. 1, this CZA monomer in dimer 1 undergoes a certain degree of rotation around the y-axis and z-axis, leading to the formation of a hydrogen bond with a bond length of 2.02 Å between two CZA monomers. The initial dimer 2 and trimer 1 consist of two and three CZA monomers stacked to form H-shaped mirror aggregates. After optimizing, the planes of the CZA monomers remain nearly parallel, and the monomers only undergo a certain relative movement in the y-axis direction and result in a decrease in interplanar spacing by 0.3 Å-0.5 Å.

J-type structures feature that CZA monomers arranged in the same plane form head-to-tail, head-to-head and tail-to-tail configurations. In order to construct J-type structures, the electrostatic repulsion among CZA monomers needs to be calculated (see Supporting Information Fig. S2). The ketone oxygen of CZA has a high nucleophilic potential, the carboxyl oxygen has a moderate nucleophilic potential, the hydroxyl oxygen has a low nucleophilic potential, and the pyridine nitrogen has a high electrophilic potential. Typical head-to-tail configurations of dimer 3 and trimer 2, head-to-head configurations of dimer 4 and dimer 5, and tail-to-tail configurations of dimer 6 are constructed by placing the electrophilic N and H atoms in relative position of the nucleophilic O atoms. The CZA molecules of J-type dimers and trimers are in the same plane, and the initial distances between neighboring H and O atoms of two molecules are in the range of 1.5 Å-2.5 Å (shown in Fig. S1). From the optimized structures of J-type aggregates, one can see that the geometry optimization does not alter the fundamental characteristics of J-type structure, but the distances between adjacent H and O atoms are reduced to a range of 1.6 Å-2.0 Å as shown in Fig. 1. The optimized dimer 3 and trimer 2 still maintain head-to-tail configuration with bond lengths of 1.61 and 1.84 Å for the OH…O and NH…O hydrogen bonds between the CZA monomers, respectively. The optimized dimer 4 and dimer 5 maintain head-to-head configurations, and both NH…O hydrogen bonds of dimer 5 exhibit the same bond length of 1.77 Å. The optimized dimer 6 maintain a tail-to-tail configuration with both OH…O hydrogen bond lengths of 1.70 Å. Based on the analyses, it is clear that there are shorter OH…O hydrogen bonds in the head-to-tail configurations. This would play a positive role in improving the stability of the J-type structures.

Next, the structures of CZA/CD aggregates are optimized. In the present work, the CDs are generated by CA and ammonia [30] and the corresponding reaction process is shown in Fig. S3. There are three reaction stages: in the first stage, citric acid and ammonia form a molecular fluorophore CZA through the amidation reaction; in the second stage, CZA, ammonia and citric acid form the CD structure through intermolecular dehydration and dehydrogenation; in the third stage, as the concentration of CZA increases, there is a gradual aggregation of CZA and CD. To clearly describe the characterization of CZA/CD aggregates, the structures are categorized into two groups according to different binding modes. The first category consists of aggregates where CZA and CD are bound together by covalent bonds, and the second category refers to the stacked structures with noncovalent bonds between CZA and CD.

For the first category, the CZA molecules and CD are covalently bonded. The naming principle of structures is “a-b-c”. Here, “a” represents the number of CZA (a = 1, 2, 3). “b” represents the binding pathway of CZA to CD (b = 1, 2, 3), where “1″ means the substitution of H or –OH at positions 1, 4, 8, 9, and 10 in the CD by the dehydrogenated N atom of CZA, ”2″ represents the de-OH of pyridine ring in CZA replacing the H atom at positions 4, 8, and 10 of CD, and “3″ denotes the dehydrogenated N atom of CZA bonded with the sp3-hybridized C atoms at positions 4, 8, and 10 of CD. ”c“ represents the binding sites of CDs (c = 1, 4, 8, 9, 10). Fig. 2 a shows the CD structure and 13 covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates optimized in this work. Taking the ”1-1-1″ structure as an example, it indicates that the dehydrogenated N atom of a CZA molecule replaces the –OH at position 1 in CD. To explore the influence of the number of CZA on the PL spectra of CDs, two different aggregates with more CZA molecules, namely 2–1–1,10 and 3–1–1,9,10, are constructed. The 2–1–1,10 configuration indicates that two N atoms of two CZA molecules are dehydrogenated to replace the –OH at position 1 and the H at position 10 of CD. The 3–1–1,9,10 configuration indicates that three N atoms of three CZA molecules are dehydrogenated to replace the –OH at positions 1 and 9 and the H at position 10 of CD.

In the second category of CZA/CD aggregates, the two components are held together by non-covalent bonds and all optimized structures are shown in Fig. 2b. In this work, six stacked structures are constructed with the naming principle “na-i”, where “n” represents non-covalent binding, “a” represents the number of CZA molecules (a = 1, 2, 3), and “i” represents the i-th structure. Here, n1-1 is the stacked structure of CZA monomer and CD, n2-1 to n2-4 represent the stacked structures by dimer 1, dimer 2, dimer 3, and dimer 6 with CD, respectively, and n3-1 represents the structure stacked by trimer 1 and CD. For all the above stacked structures, the initial interplanar distances between CZA and CD are 3 Å-4 Å (see Fig. S1). After structural optimization, the CZA and CD molecules do not rotate distinctly, and there is only relative translation between the molecules along the y-axis direction. As shown in Fig. 2, the interplanar distances between CZA and CD molecules are reduced, with the largest reduction observed in n2-2 and n3-1 structures, where hydrogen bonds with a bond length of 1.8 Å are formed between the CZA molecules and CD.

To assess the thermodynamic stability of CZA and CZA/CD aggregates, the cohesion energies of all structures are calculated [45,46]. If the cohesion energy is negative, meaning that the structure is stable. The lower the cohesion energy of the structure, the more stable it is. The formula for calculating the cohesion energy is given below:

where E coh is the cohesion energy of the aggregated structure, E CDs is the total energy of the CZA or CZA/CD aggregates, E c and E x are the energies of C and X (X = N, O, H) atoms, respectively, and N c and N x are the number of C and X atoms, respectively.

The cohesion energies of all the structures are given in Table 1 . It can be seen that the cohesion energies of all CZA and CZA/CD aggregates are negative. This indicates that these structures can be stabilized. In order to further discuss the structural stability of the aggregates and figure out the interactions within the aggregates, the weak interactions are visualized using the independent gradient model based on Hirshfeld partition (IGMH) method [51]. The result is illustrated with coloring in Fig. S4.

The structural stability of the CZA aggregates is analyzed at first. In Table 1, for the H-type CZA dimers, dimer 1 has a lower cohesion energy than dimer 2, indicating that the anti-mirror stacking structure is more stable. From the visualized weak interactions diagram (as shown in Fig. S5), it is observed that the OH…O hydrogen bonding is generated in dimer 1, while dimer 2 only has van der Waals force interactions. These observations indicate that the hydrogen bonding interactions in dimer 1 contribute to its enhanced structural stability. Of all the CZA aggregates, the J-type dimer 3 with head-to-tail configuration has the lowest cohesion energy. This structure is the most stable one due to the short bond length (1.61 Å) of OH…O hydrogen bonding and the strong interaction between the CZA monomers. The NH…O hydrogen bond formed in dimer 4 has the longest bond length and the interactions between the CZA molecules are weak, making the structure less stable. For the trimeric structures of CZA, trimer 1 exhibits H-type structural characteristics, while trimer 2 exhibits J-type characteristics. The latter has a lower cohesion energy, suggesting that J-type trimer has a more stable structure. Based on the cohesion energies of all CZA oligomers, all J-type structures display high stability, and the J-type head-to-tail configuration is the most stable.

Different from the aggregates of CZA itself, the structural stability of CZA/CD aggregates is related not only to the aggregation configuration of CZA, but also to the bonding mode between the two components. Among the six noncovalently bonded CZA/CD structures, n2-1 and n2-2 are formed by stacking CD with dimer 1 and 2, respectively. Originally, dimer 1 has a lower cohesion energy than dimer 2. However, when the dimers are bound with CD, the cohesion energy of n2-2 structure is 0.06 kcal mol−1 lower than that of n2-1. Based on the weak interaction analysis (see Fig. S5), there is only van der Waals forces between dimer 1 and CD in n2-1, while the hydrogen bonding exists between dimer 2 and CD in n2-2. These indicate that hydrogen bonding is beneficial for improving the structural stability of n2-2 aggregate. Among all noncovalently bonded CZA/CD stacked structures, the n2-3 structure formed by CD and J-type dimer 3 with head-to-tail configuration has the lowest cohesion energy. This suggests that J-type head-to-tail CZA oligomers are more prone to form stable stacked structures with CD.

Compared to above noncovalently bonded structures, the cohesion energies of CZA/CD aggregates decrease significantly when CZA molecules are covalently bonded to CD. This is attributed to the fact that the effect of covalent bonds is much greater than the weak interactions arising from non-covalent bonds. Among all the covalently bonded structures, the lowest cohesion energy is found in the 1–2-8 structure, followed by the 1–2-4 and 1–2-10 structures. The three structures share a common feature that CZA achieves the covalent bonding with CD by substituting the H atom of CD by de-OH on the pyridine ring. This suggests that the structural stability of covalently bonded aggregates is related to the binding sites. Combined with the calculated cohesion energy, it can be predicted that when CZA and CD coexist in solution, the covalent bonds between CZA and CD are most likely to be formed through the substitution of the H atom at the edge of CD by the de-OH of pyridine ring in CZA.

Fig. 3 a shows the PL spectra of the CZA monomer, dimers and trimers. To compare the spectral shift before and after the formation of aggregates, the calculated emission peak of CZA monomer is corrected for 60 nm red-shift based on the PL spectral data measured in experiments [30,52]. The results show that compared to the emission peak of CZA monomer, those of the J-type dimers and trimers do not vary significantly, and the peak positions only shift by 0–12 nm. The PL spectra of head-to-tail configurations, namely dimer 3 and trimer 2, almost entirely overlap with that of the CZA monomer. The inset of Fig. 3a shows the emission peaks of H-type dimer 1 and dimer 2 are located at 579 and 594 nm, respectively. In comparison to the CZA monomer, the emission peaks of dimer 1 and dimer 2 are red-shifted by 138 and 154 nm, respectively. However, the luminescence intensities of these two dimers are extremely low and the oscillator strength is only 0.003, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching occurs for both H-type dimers. In addition, H-type dimers tend to exhibit larger Stokes shift compared to J-type dimers, as shown in Table S1.

Analyzing S 1-S 0 transitions for the emission peaks of all CZA aggregates, one can see that the primary contribution is made by HOMO to LUMO electron transitions, with configuration interaction contributions ranging from 97 % to 99 % (see Table S1). Therefore, to further illustrate the luminescence properties of CZA aggregates, the absorption spectra of CZA monomer, dimers, and trimers are shown in Fig. S6a. As the number of CZA increases, the intensity of the absorption peak at 340 nm gradually enhances and a blue shift occurs, which is in accordance with the existing experimental results [30,34].

Fig. 3b shows the PL spectra of the noncovalently bonded CZA/CD stacked structures. Compared to the emission peak of CD, those of the stacked structures only undergo slight shifts, and the variations are shown in Table S2. The PL spectrum of n2-1 structure stacked by H-type dimer 1 and CD is red-shifted by 8 nm. The structures n2-2 and n3-1, formed by the binding of CD to H-type dimer 2 and trimer 1, respectively, exhibit a blue shift of 12 nm in their PL spectra. The PL spectra of J-type dimers stacked with CD are almost not shifted. The results indicate that the emission spectra of noncovalently bonded aggregates are primarily influenced by the aggregation configuration of CZA. When H-type CZA oligomers are bound to CD, the PL spectra of aggregates only exhibit small shifts. When J-type structures are bound to CD, there is hardly any shift observed in the PL spectra. Many experiments have found that CDs synthesized from citric acid and ammonia/urea show dual emission, and the green emission is mainly contributed by CDs [21,32], which is in agreement with the calculated PL spectra. Moreover, the PL intensities of all the noncovalently bonded structures are slightly lower than that of a separate CD. The calculated absorption spectra of these structures (shown in Fig. S6b) reveal that as the number of CZA increases, the absorption peaks located near 350 nm are blue shifted. Recently, Cappai et al. experimentally observed a blue-shift of the absorption peak around 350 nm with increasing CZA concentration [34], and the present calculations are in good agreement with the experimental observation. This suggests that the noncovalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates are not responsible for the high fluorescence intensity of CD induced by CZA.

In order to better analyze the PL spectra of covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates, all the structures are divided into two groups according to the degree of spectral shift. The first group exhibits a significant spectrum shift, corresponding to the CZA/CD aggregates with binding sites “1″, ”4″ and “9″. The second group only displays a slight spectrum shift, corresponding to structures with binding sites ”8″ and “10″. Fig. 3c shows the PL spectra of the first group of CZA/CD aggregates. One can see that the PL spectra of these structures undergo a remarkable red-shift or blue-shift compared to a single CD. When one CZA molecule and two CZA molecules are covalently bonded with CD to form 1–1–1 and 2–1–1,10 structures, the PL spectra exhibit blue-shifts of 18 and 32 nm, respectively. In the two cases, the electron-donor group (–OH) at position 1 in CD is substituted by the dehydrogenated N atom of CZA, leading to a blue-shift in the spectrum. However, when three CZA molecules are bonded with CD, the 3–1–1,9,10 structure undergoes a fluorescence quenching, as shown in the inset in Fig. 3c. The result tells that as the number of CZA molecules increases, the distance between the CZA molecules decreases, making the CZA/CD aggregates more prone to fluorescence quenching. The PL spectra of other four covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates show a red-shift within a range of 15 to 133 nm. Among these, the fluorescence spectrum of the 1–2-4 structure exhibits the greatest red-shift. In this structure, there is a coplanar arrangement between CZA and CD, which enhances the effective conjugation length of the CD, and thus the PL spectrum exhibits the most significant red shift. Combined with the calculated absorption spectra (see Fig. S6c), it is found that the 1–2-4 structure displays the most pronounced absorption peak from 400 to 450 nm, thus the PL spectral intensity of the 1–2-4 structure appears to be prominently enhanced. Besides, the red-shifted PL emission peaks of 1–1-4, 1–2-4 and 1–3-4 structures change more significantly than the first absorption peaks, leading to the larger Stokes shifts, as shown in Table S2.

The PL spectra of covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates with binding sites “8″ and ”10″ are given in Fig. 3d. It is found that there is almost no shift in the PL spectra and little change occurs in the spectral intensity of these structures compared to that of a single CD. Also, the oscillator strength of these structures does not change significantly (see Table S2), and the calculated absorption peaks of these structures with the same binding site are very close, for instance, the absorption spectra of 1–1-8 and 1–3-8 almost overlap as shown in Fig. S6d.

The above discussion manifests that the binding sites of CZA and CD play a crucial role in influencing the positions of the emission peaks of the covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates. The electronic characteristics of the substituted atoms or functional groups at the binding sites will be fully described in Section 3.4.

In addition to PL spectral shift, the spectrum intensity is also an important physical quantity for the PL properties of CDs. Comparing the PL intensities of all CZA/CD aggregates in Fig. 3b-d, it can be found that for the noncovalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates, CZA weakens the PL intensity of CD due to noncovalent bonding interactions such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, etc. For the covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates, there are two different cases. When the CZA molecule is located at the binding positions in non-conjugated graphitic nitrogen heterocycles, CZA has no essential effect on the PL strength of CD. However, when the binding site belongs to an electron-donor group, an electron-withdrawing group, or the edge of sp2 carbon domain, CZA can produce a dominant enhancement of the PL strength of CD. This conclusion rationally explains the observed enhancement of QYs resulting from the interaction between CZA and CDs [33] and offers a theoretical guidance for enhancing the QYs of CD by using MFs.

Furthermore, many experimental works observed that the blue-green dual emission of CDs formed by CA and ammonia [21,30]. According to the PL peak positions of all CZA aggregates and CZA/CD aggregates in Fig. 3a-d, it is concluded that the blue emission is mainly attributed to the formation of CZA oligomers such as dimers or trimers, while the green emission is mainly attributed to CD or the aggregation of CZA and CD. In fact, all the CZA oligomers, CZA/CD aggregates, and CD may coexist in solution. The aggregation states of CZA/CD and the PL properties of CD are closely related to the experimental conditions such as the ratio and concentration of raw materials. If the concentration of CZA in the solution is relatively high, the distance between CZA molecules decreases, and it is easy to form H-type or J-type CZA aggregates. If the concentrations of CZA and CD are high, they will form covalently bonded or noncovalently bonded aggregates. In addition, the dual emission of CDs is wavelength-dependent, which mainly comes from the aggregation states of CZA/CD. As shown in Fig. S6 and Tables S1, S2, the absorption spectra of CZA aggregates are mainly distributed in the range of 200 ∼ 400 nm, and the absorption spectra of CZA/CD aggregates are distributed in the range of 200 ∼ 430 nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength less than 400 nm mainly excites CZA aggregates. For example, when the excitation wavelength is 300 nm, the H-type CZA dimer exhibits one more peak than the J-type CZA dimer, indicating that this wavelength mainly excites the H-type dimer. Some experiments have found that the PL intensity of CDs decreases when the excitation wavelength is 300 nm [30]. Our theoretical findings provide an explanation for the underlying physical nature of this experimental phenomenon, which can be attributed to the predominant excitation of the H-type dimer by the 300 nm excitation wavelength, and H-type dimers are known to be prone to fluorescence quenching. As a result, when the excitation wavelength is 300 nm, the PL intensity of CDs decreases. When the excitation wavelength is greater than 400 nm, the CZA/CD aggregates are mainly excited.

It is worth mentioning that fluorescence quenching occurs in both CZA and CZA/CD aggregates. In order to explore the underlying reason for fluorescence quenching, the weak interactions of H-type dimer 1, dimer 2 and 3–1-1,9,10 structures in the excited state are analyzed (see Fig. S7). One can see that complete ring isosurfaces are formed between the CZA molecules of these three structures, corresponding to π-π stacking [53]. In contrast, for the H-type 2–1-1,10 structure, the distance between the CZA molecules is 4.06 Å, thus no typical π-π stacking is formed and the fluorescence quenching does not occur. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CZA aggregates and CZA/CD aggregates is mainly caused by the π-π stacking effect between the CZA molecules, which is closely related to the distance between the CZA molecules [54]. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is further discussed in Section 3.5.

Fig. 4 shows the frontier molecular orbitals energy levels of CZA aggregates. Here, HOMO level means the highest occupied molecular orbital, and LUMO level means the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. In Fig. 4, one can see that compared to the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of CZA monomer, those of H-type dimer 1 and dimer 2 are lowered by 0.98 and 1.09 eV, respectively. The orbital transitions are changed from a single CZA to two CZAs, but the transitions still essentially belong to the π-π* transitions. For J-type dimers or trimers, the energy gaps change less and the orbital transitions still primarily involve a single CZA molecule, which is a typical π-π* transition. The results show that compared with the monomer, the π-π stacking interaction between CZA molecules leads to the delocalization of the molecular orbital to the entire H-type dimer, and the energy gap decreases, resulting in red shift of PL spectrum and lager Stokes shift. In comparison, for J-type dimers, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding has a slight effect on the molecular orbital and energy gap, leading to a small change in PL spectrum and Stokes shift.

Fig. 5 shows the frontier molecular orbital energy levels for the noncovalently bonded CZA/CD stacked structures. The energy gaps of these structures show only slight changes compared to that of a single CD. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap of n2-1 structure only decreases by 0.11 eV, and those of n2-2 and n3-1 structures increase by 0.04 and 0.03 eV, respectively. As shown in the figure, the contribution of the molecular orbitals of these CZA/CD stacked structures is primarily from the π-π* orbital transitions of CD. The result suggests that when H-type or J-type CZA molecules and CD undergo the stacked aggregation without forming covalent bonding, the electronic orbitals are still mainly distributed on CD. The change of frontier molecular orbitals energy levels of these structures is consistent with the absence of significant shifts in their PL spectra shown in Fig. 3b.

For covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels and molecular orbital distributions depend on the binding site of CZA and CD. The frontier molecular orbital diagrams of the aggregates with binding sites “1″, ”4″, and “9″ are shown in Fig. 6 a. Compared to the HOMO-LUMO gap of CD, those of 1–1–1 and 2–1–1,10 structures are enlarged by 0.05 and 0.14 eV, respectively, thus leading to a blue-shift of the emission peaks. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap for the 1–2-4 structure experiences a noteworthy reduction of 0.70 eV. Observing the molecular orbital distribution of this structure, it is found that the electrons have delocalized from the CD to the CZA, and there is an increase in the contribution of the π-π* transition orbitals. This leads to a large Stokes shift and a red shift observed in the PL spectrum. Similar phenomena are observed in the HOMO-LUMO energy gaps and molecular orbitals of 1–1-4, 1–3-4, and 1–1-9 structures. Fig. 6b shows the frontier molecular orbital and energy level diagrams for the CZA/CD aggregates with binding sites ”8″ and “10″. The energy gaps of these structures vary only in the range of 0–0.03 eV, and the molecular orbital transitions are mainly attributed to π-π* transitions of CD, with almost no contribution from positions 8 and 10. This explains that no obvious shift in the PL spectra occurs for this class of structures.

According to the above molecular orbital distribution, the influence of binding sites on the PL properties of CZA/CD aggregates is analyzed in details. The –OH at position 1 of CD belongs to the electron-donor group and contributes more to the molecular orbital before covalent bonding forms. When CZA molecules and CD are covalently bonded at position 1, the –OH group is replaced, causing a decrease in the electron distribution of CD and then a blue-shift in the PL spectrum. The pyridine ring where the H at position 4 is located belongs to the sp2 carbon domain. When the CZA molecule forms a covalent bond with CD at position 4, a new electron channel is established between CZA and CD. This corresponds to an expansion of the sp2 domain, and results in a red-shift in the PL spectrum. The –COOH at position 9 belongs to the electron-withdrawing group. When the CZA molecule replaces –COOH at position 9, the electrons delocalize towards the side of CZA, resulting in a red-shift in the PL spectrum. In a word, when the CZA molecules are bonded to positions 1, 4, or 9 of CD, the delocalization of electrons from CD to CZA takes place, and CZA is actually involved in the luminescence process. In these cases, the HOMO-LUMO gaps undergo significant changes, and thus the emission spectra of these structures are distinctly shifted. In contrast, when the CZA molecules are bonded to positions 8 and 10 with no orbital contribution, it cannot radically change the original molecular orbital distribution of CD, and thus the aggregation of CZA has almost no essential effect on the PL spectra of CDs.
ii)
Transition dipole moments

In order to emphasize the influence of the aggregation configuration on the PL intensity, the total transition dipole moments (TDM) of all CZA/CD aggregates are calculated as shown in Fig. 7 . The corresponding vector diagrams of TDM are given in Fig. S8. The results show that the total TDM of CD is primarily contributed by the pyridine ring. The total TDM (orange squares in Fig. 7) of covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates with binding sites “1″, ”4″, and “9″ are notably enlarged. When CZA replaces the electron-donating group (–OH) at position 1 of CD, it leads to a decrease in the TDM of the pyridine ring, a significant increase in the graphite nitrogen heterocycles, and increase in the total TDM and oscillation strength. Thus, the probability of radiative transition of CDs is increased. When CZA replaces the H atom at position 4 of CD, the TDM of the pyridine ring increases significantly, resulting in an increase in the total TDM. Especially, this increase in 1–2-4 structure is the most notable with the total TDM of 7.125. When CZA replaces the electron-withdrawing group (–COOH) at position 9 of CD, the TDM of the pyridine ring decreases along the y-axis, but that of the graphite nitrogen heterocycles increases in the same direction. This results in a remarkable increase in the total TDM. As shown in Fig. 7, the total TDM (blue squares) for the covalently bonded CZA/CD with binding sites of ”8″ and “10″ are basically unchanged. Neither the fragmentary nor the total TDM of these structures change significantly. For noncovalently bonded CZA/CD structures, only CD plays a major role in the total TDM (purple squares). In addition, there is a special 3–1-1,9,10 structure, and its total TDM (red squares) is almost zero. In this structure, the TDM mostly comes from the two CZA molecules bonded at positions 9 and 10, but the two TDMs in opposite directions essentially cancel each other out, leading to the fluorescence quenching. In addition, experiments have found that N doping will form N-related molecular centers and surface centers, resulting in a decrease in the relative contribution of O-containing functional groups and an increase in the relative contribution of CC. As a result, the QY of CDs is enhanced [37].

In order to analyze the effect of CZA containing N on CD, Fig. S8 shows the atomic TDM of the covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates with binding sites “1″, ”4″ and “9″. The results show that when CZA molecule replaces the –OH at position 1 or the –COOH at position 9 of CD, it is equivalent to reducing the content of O-containing functional groups of CD and increasing the relative contribution of C atoms in graphite N heterocyclic fragments, resulting in enhanced PL intensity, such as 1–1–1 and 1–1-9 structures. When CZA and CD are covalently bonded at site 4, the relative contribution of C atoms in the pyridine ring fragment is increased, resulting in enhanced PL intensity, such as 1–2-4. This result reveals the essential reason why the covalent bond aggregation of CZA and CD enhances the QYs of N-doped CDs.

The above analysis shows that when the binding site of CZA molecules and CD belongs to the electron-donating group, electron-withdrawing group, or sp2 domain, the TDM of the covalently bonded CZA/CD structure would be significantly increased. This leads to the enhanced fluorescence intensity. When the binding site of CZA molecules and CD belongs to non-conjugated graphitic nitrogen heterocycle, the total TDM of the CZA/CD aggregate remains almost unchanged, and correspondingly the fluorescence intensity remains almost unchanged. The noncovalently bonded CZA/CD structure has a significantly reduced TDM, which causes a reduced fluorescence intensity. In particular, when two CZA fragments produce two TDMs in opposite directions, the total TDM would be approximately equal to zero and therefore the fluorescence quenching occurs.

Fluorescence quenching is a common occurrence in CDs synthesis experiments, and it is often speculated that fluorescence quenching depends on the distance between particles [54]. To further explore the underlying nature of fluorescence quenching, three H-type CZA dimers were constructed. Here, the initial interplanar spacing between the stacking monomers of three structures are set to 3.5 Å. From dimer 7 to dimer 9, the initial distances between the centers of the pyridine rings are 5.2, 4.1, and 3.4 Å, respectively. All the structures are optimized, and thus the intermolecular distances are reduced to 2.95, 3.04, and 2.89 Å, and the distances between the centers of the pyridine ring are reduced to 3.42, 3.18 and 2.99 Å, respectively, shown as Fig. 8 . The distance between the pyridine rings is less than 3.5 Å, which can be attributed to the van der Waals radius of a carbon atom ranging between 1.72 and 1.80 Å [53]. As the distance between the centers of the pyridine rings decreases, the pyridine rings overlap more, and the van der Waals forces between the CZA molecules act more strongly. In particular, a wide and flat complete ring-shaped isosurface is formed between the two CZA molecules of dimer 9, leading to π-π stacking [51]. Based on the PL spectra of the three dimers shown in Fig. 9 a, it can be confirmed that as the distance between the pyridine ring centers decreases, the degree of overlap of the pyridine rings intensifies, and thus the PL spectra gradually red-shift and fluorescence quenching. Fig. 9b shows the HOMO-LUMO energy levels of the three dimers. The results indicate that as the intermolecular distances decrease, the π-π stacking interaction can lead to a decrease in the energy gap and the red shift of PL spectrum. Combining with the absorption spectra shown in Fig. S9, it is found that the change of the first absorption peak is relatively small, a significant red shift in the PL spectrum results in a large Stokes shift.

To further analyze the stacking properties of these dimers, Fig. S10 demonstrates the HOMO orbital overlap and LUMO orbital overlap of the dimer molecules. As can be seen from the figure, as the overlap intensity of orbitals between CZA molecules increases, the contribution of antibonding orbitals becomes more significant, resulting in a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. In particular, when the distance between the pyridine rings decreases to 3 Å, the LUMO orbital overlap is predominantly in opposing phase, leading to the forbidden transition and annihilation of luminous intensity.

Fig. 10 analyzes the TDMs of three dimer configurations. It can be seen that compared to the total TDM of single CZA, those of the two CZA molecules in dimer 7 begin to decrease. The change in TDM of H-type dimers is related to the overlapping degree of pyridine rings of CZA. As the centers of two pyridine rings gradually approach, the total TDM of the dimer decreases, and thus the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. When the interplanar spacing between CZA molecules is less than 3 Å, the TDMs of the two molecules cancel each other out, leading to the forbidden transition of CZA or CZA/CD aggregates.

In this work, the influence of the aggregation configuration of CZA and CD on the photoluminescence of CDs has been investigated using DFT and TD-DFT methods. Structural stability, weak interactions, frontier molecular orbitals, and transition dipole moments are analyzed for a series of CZA aggregates and CZA/CD aggregates.

When CZA and CD form aggregates, the bonding mode and bonding site have a significant impact on the PL properties of CDs. The covalently bonded CZA/CD aggregates have more stable structures than the noncovalently bonded ones. When the binding site of CZA molecules with CDs belongs to the electron donor, electron acceptor, or sp2 domain, a significant shift in the PL spectrum of CDs and a noticeable enhancement in the PL intensity would be induced. The formation of CZA oligomers mainly contributes to the blue emission, while the aggregation of CZA and CD mainly contributes to the green emission.

This work gives a deep understanding of the interaction between CZA and CD in solution, helps to explain enhanced fluorescent intensity, dual emission and the fluorescence quenching caused by molecular fluorophore CZA and provides a theoretical guidance for the future development of CDs in the field of optoelectronics.
